courses.title: Strength of Materials
courses.summary: Stress where it lives: axial members, torsion, bending, combined loading, and failure theories — the course that turns statics diagrams into the numbers written on repair procedures and fitness-for-service notes.

lessons.t: Stress and strain under axial load
lessons.scope: Normal stress and strain defined from first principles; the tensile curve reread as an engineer's contract — modulus, yield, ultimate, ductility. Allowable stress and factor of safety as institutional honesty. Saint-Venant's caution about where formulas apply.
preview: A 16 mm bar carries 45 kN: compute the stress and the safety factor against 355 MPa yield.
Engineering versus true stress: which keeps rising after necking, and why?
Why do aluminum alloys need the 0.2% offset construction to define yield at all?

lessons.scope: Deformation of stepped and composite bars. Statically indeterminate axial problems. Thermal expansion against restraint — the piping stress problem in embryo.
Shear stress and angle of twist in circular shafts derived. Power–torque–speed relations for drivelines. Sizing a pump shaft and reading a torsional failure surface's 45° signature.
The flexure formula derived from plane sections. Section modulus as the designer's currency. Where beams actually fail — and why the extreme fiber earns its name.
Shear stress distribution and shear flow. Why webs carry shear and flanges carry moment in an I-beam. Built-up sections and their fastener spacing.
Elastic curve by integration; superposition with standard cases. Stiffness as a serviceability limit distinct from strength. Machinery baseplate and walkway deflection checks.
Axial, torsion, bending, and pressure superposed at a point. Critical-point hunting on real components. A shaft with a pulley worked completely — the pattern for machine design.
Plane-stress transformation derived, then Mohr's circle as its graphical engine. Principal stresses and maximum shear located. Why fatigue cracks pick their planes.
Hoop and axial stress derived from equilibrium. The 2:1 hoop-to-axial ratio and what it predicts about vessel failure orientation. First contact with the equipment class Asset Integrity will govern.
Tresca and von Mises for ductile metals; maximum normal stress for brittle ones. Using them on combined-stress states from lesson 7. What 'the stress' means when there are six components.
Euler buckling derived; effective length and slenderness. The crossover between yielding and buckling. Why long slender compression members fail sideways without warning.
Kt at holes, fillets, and keyways; the geometry tax on nominal stress. Notch sensitivity in brief. The handshake with Materials II's fatigue lesson — geometry and metallurgy jointly set life.

courses.title: Thermodynamics II — Cycles & Utilities
courses.summary: The plant utilities course wearing a thermodynamics badge: steam cycles, gas turbines, refrigeration, compressed air, and psychrometrics — sized, audited, and read from the utility deck of a real factory.
taught_from: Çengel-tradition cycle analysis via MIT OCW 2.005/2.006 materials

lessons.scope: The ideal Rankine cycle on T–s coordinates with each device audited by the steady-flow energy equation. Efficiency levers: pressure, superheat, condenser vacuum. The backbone of every steam utility system.

lessons.t: Real steam cycles: reheat, regeneration, losses
lessons.scope: Reheat and feedwater heating and why plants pay for them. Component irreversibilities located on the h–s diagram. Heat rate as the operator's efficiency language.

Boilers, headers, PRVs, steam traps, and condensate return as one thermodynamic system. Steam quality, flash steam, and trap failure modes costed with the SFEE. The utilities lesson KDD-scale food plants and EQUATE-scale process plants both live by.
preview: A trap fails open on a 10 bar header: name the two distinct loss mechanisms it creates.
Why is hot condensate worth far more returned than its water value alone?
Explain flash steam: why does condensate partially vaporize crossing into low pressure?

lessons.scope: Brayton cycle with real component efficiencies. Pressure ratio trade-offs; intercooling and regeneration in outline. The prime mover behind Gulf power and large compression trains.
The refrigeration cycle on P–h coordinates; COP computed from enthalpies. Superheat and subcooling as health indicators. Chillers and cold stores — refrigeration as a food plant's beating heart.
Moist-air properties and the psychrometric chart worked fluently. Cooling-and-dehumidification processes traced. Gulf-summer design conditions and what they do to equipment ratings.
Compression work, multistaging, and intercooling derived and priced. Receivers, dryers, and dew point. Leak economics — why compressed air is the plant's most expensive utility per joule.
Stoichiometry, excess air, and flue-gas analysis. Heating values and combustion efficiency from stack measurements. Boiler tuning as applied chemistry.
Second-law accounting applied to boilers, heat exchangers, and throttles. Exergy destruction rankings as an audit's priority list. Where the utility bill actually leaks.

A guided paper audit of a representative factory utility deck: steam, air, chilled water. Balances closed, losses ranked, fixes proposed with payback estimates. The Tier-2 utilities lesson practiced end to end.

courses.title: Fluid Mechanics
courses.summary: Pressure, flow, and loss: statics to Bernoulli to real pipe networks with pumps attached — taught so that a pump curve, a system curve, and an NPSH margin become native reading.
taught_from: Çengel & Cimbala, Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and Applications (McGraw-Hill)

lessons.scope: Density, viscosity, vapor pressure, surface tension — each tied to a plant consequence. Newtonian behavior and where slurries and polymers depart from it. Vapor pressure flagged early: cavitation is coming.
Pressure fields, manometry, and forces on submerged surfaces. Gauge versus absolute pressure — the eternal instrumentation trap. Tank and dam wall calculations done cleanly.
Streamlines, flow regimes, and the Reynolds number as the master dimensionless group. Laminar and turbulent flow distinguished physically. Where transition sits in real pipe sizes.
Control-volume mass balances, steady and unsteady. Velocity–area trades in ducts and nozzles. The one-line equation that gating design already used in MFG 105.

lessons.t: Bernoulli, energy equation, pipe flow, and pump curves
lessons.scope: Bernoulli derived with its assumptions on the table, then upgraded to the energy equation with head loss and pump head. Major losses via friction factor and the Moody chart; minor losses by coefficient. System curve meets pump curve — the operating point found, the lesson this whole course exists to deliver.
preview: Static head 12 m plus 6 m friction at design flow: what pump head is needed, and what happens if friction doubles?
Why does the pump-curve/system-curve intersection define a stable operating point?
In turbulent pipe flow, head loss scales with roughly which power of velocity — and what does that mean for 'just open the valve more'?

lessons.scope: Control-volume momentum for forces on bends, nozzles, and vanes. Thrust blocks and pipe anchor loads. Why elbows in large lines need restraint calculations.
Buckingham Pi with engineering judgment. Model testing and scale-up logic. The affinity-law preview: why pump performance scales the way it does.

lessons.t: Laminar and turbulent pipe flow in depth
lessons.scope: Fully developed laminar flow derived to the Hagen–Poiseuille result. Turbulent velocity profiles and roughness effects. Entrance lengths and where gauges should not be installed.
Series and parallel lines; three-reservoir style problems by iteration. Valve types and their loss and control characters. Reading a pump-and-piping isometric as a resistance network.

Orifice, venturi, rotameter, magnetic, ultrasonic, and Coriolis meters — principle, accuracy, and installation effects. Differential-pressure metering derived from Bernoulli. Straight-run requirements as physics, not superstition.
Euler turbomachine equation derived from angular momentum. Velocity triangles for a centrifugal impeller. Specific speed as the shape parameter of the pump world.
Mach number, choking, and converging-nozzle behavior. Relief and blowdown implications. Enough gas dynamics to respect what happens downstream of a PSV.

courses.title: Metrology & Quality Control
courses.summary: Measurement you can defend: instruments, uncertainty, calibration, GD&T verification, and the SPC toolkit — the quality half of a production engineer's day job.
taught_from: NPTEL: Metrology and Quality Control-style courses (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Accuracy, precision, resolution, and bias untangled for good. Traceability chains to national standards. The vocabulary that makes calibration certificates readable.
Calipers, micrometers, dial indicators, gauge blocks, and comparators — use, care, and abuse. Abbe error and why instrument geometry matters. Temperature effects at the micron level.
Uncertainty budgets built from type A and B components. Calibration intervals and out-of-tolerance investigations. A micrometer calibration worked end to end.
Measuring flatness, roundness, and runout — the rotating-equipment repair set. Verifying position with datums. CMM measurement in outline.
Ra, Rz, and the stylus trace read properly. Cutoffs and filtering honestly explained. Finish specs matched to gaskets, seals, and bearing seats.
Variation: common versus special cause — the distinction all of SPC rests on. X̄–R charts built and read. Control limits versus specification limits, never confused again.

lessons.t: Statistical process control II and capability
lessons.scope: Attribute charts in brief; capability indices Cp and Cpk computed and abused-proofed. What Cpk = 1.33 actually promises. Capability studies as pre-production gatekeeping.
Sampling plans, OC curves, and the risks each party carries. AQL semantics without the folklore. Where sampling makes sense and where 100% inspection is owed.
Repeatability and reproducibility studies: can the gauge see the tolerance? %GRR computed and judged. The study that should precede every SPC deployment and rarely does.
ISO 9001 logic: say it, do it, prove it. Nonconformance, CAPA, and audit trails. Where quality systems touch maintenance records — more often than either side admits.

courses.title: Electronics & Sensors
courses.summary: From signal to decision: analog conditioning, digital basics, and the industrial sensor families — temperature, pressure, flow, level, proximity, vibration — wired, scaled, and sanity-checked on a production line.

lessons.scope: Diode behavior and rectification; regulated DC supplies that feed every control panel. Ripple and regulation specs read from a datasheet. The 24 VDC world introduced.
The transistor as a switch; driving relays and indicator loads. Saturation, cutoff, and heat. Solid-state relays versus electromechanical ones.
Amplification, filtering, and instrumentation amplifiers for small sensor signals. Common-mode rejection as noise defense. Why thermocouple microvolts survive a noisy plant at all.
Logic levels, gates, and simple combinational thinking. Discrete inputs and outputs as the PLC sees them. Sinking versus sourcing — the wiring confusion resolved once.

The industrial sensor families in one working tour — RTD/thermocouple, pressure transmitters, flow and level, proximity and photoelectric — each with its signal, failure modes, and wiring. The 4–20 mA loop derived as an engineering decision, live zero included. A filling line's sensor set walked end to end.
preview: A 4–20 mA transmitter spans 0–5 m of level and reads 12 mA: what is the level?
Why is the live zero at 4 mA instead of 0 mA?
RTD or thermocouple for a 90 °C bearing housing: which, and on what grounds?

lessons.scope: RTD classes and self-heating; thermocouple types, cold junctions, and extension wire sins. Thermowells and response lag. Choosing and installing temperature points that tell the truth.

lessons.t: Pressure and level measurement in depth
lessons.scope: Transmitter anatomy: sensing, isolation, and impulse lines. DP level measurement with wet legs — where the arithmetic goes wrong. Purges, seals, and Gulf-summer condensation traps.
Sampling, aliasing, and resolution in bits. Anti-alias filtering as a non-negotiable. Why a vibration channel's sample rate sets what defects it can see — the data-collector connection.
Grounding, shielding, and segregation of signal from power. Ground loops diagnosed and killed. HART over 4–20 mA in outline.

Specify the measurement points for a small process skid: variables, technologies, ranges, and installation details. Datasheets filled honestly. The exercise instrument engineers call 'doing the index'.

courses.title: Engineering Statistics
courses.summary: Data with judgment: probability, distributions, estimation, hypothesis tests, and regression — the quiet prerequisite under SPC, Weibull analysis, measurement uncertainty, and every reliability claim you will ever audit.
taught_from: MIT OCW 18.05 Introduction to Probability and Statistics (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.scope: Location, spread, and shape; histograms and box plots that don't lie. Outliers treated as evidence, not embarrassment. Plant data pathologies previewed.
Sample spaces, independence, and conditional probability. Bayes' theorem worked on an inspection-alarm example. The base-rate trap that fools test results everywhere.
Binomial and Poisson models with their assumptions audited. Defect counts and rare-event arrivals. When the Poisson approximation earns its keep.

The normal distribution and its z-arithmetic; lognormal for repair times; exponential for memoryless failure. Choosing a model from data shape and physics. The exponential's constant hazard flagged for Reliability I.
Why averages behave better than individuals. Standard error and the central limit theorem demonstrated numerically. The statistical footing under control charts.
Point estimates and confidence intervals for means and proportions. Interpretation without the classic misreadings. Sample-size arithmetic before the test campaign, not after.

Null logic, p-values, and both error types with plant-decision stakes. One- and two-sample t-tests. 'Statistically significant' versus 'operationally meaningful' — a distinction with budget consequences.
Least squares fitting with residual diagnostics. Interpretation of slope, intercept, and R² without overclaiming. Degradation trends and calibration curves as the working examples.
One factor at a time versus factorial thinking. Main effects and interactions read from a 2² experiment. The efficiency argument that Lean Six Sigma will later monetize.
Censored data — the defining feature of failure records. Hazard and survival functions introduced gently. The bridge lesson into Weibull analysis in Reliability I.
P-hacking, survivorship bias, and cherry-picked baselines — recognized and named. Uncertainty statements as professional obligation. How to present data to management without lying by chart.
